name: CBS domain
description: CBS domains are evolutionarily conserved structural domains found in a variety of non functionally-related proteins from all kingdoms of life. These domains pair together to form a intramolecular dimeric structure (CBS pair), termed Bateman domain [[cite:PUB00000457], [cite:PUB00043686], [cite:PUB00099760], [cite:PUB00019267]]. CBS domains have been shown to bind mainly ligands with an adenosyl group such as AMP, ATP and S-AdoMet, but may also bind metal ions, or nucleic acids [[cite:PUB00099760], [cite:PUB00014845]]. Hence, they play an essential role in the regulation of the activities of numerous proteins, and mutations in them are associated with several hereditary diseases [[cite:PUB00099758], [cite:PUB00043686], [cite:PUB00014845]]. CBS domains are found attached to a wide range of other protein domains suggesting that CBS domains may play a regulatory role making proteins sensitive to adenosyl-carrying ligands. The region containing the CBS domains in cystathionine-beta synthase is involved in regulation by S-AdoMet [[cite:PUB00019266]]. CBS domain pairs from AMPK bind AMP or ATP [[cite:PUB00014845]]. The CBS domains from IMPDH, which bind ATP, have shown to have a role in the regulation of adenylate nucleotide synthesis [[cite:PUB00014845], [cite:PUB00099759]].

name: ABC transporter-like, ATP-binding domain
description:
This entry represents a conserved region found in a group of related ATP-binding proteins and is associated with ATP-binding [[cite:PUB00002368], [cite:PUB00000705], [cite:PUB00003995], [cite:PUB00003996], [cite:PUB00003805]].

The proteins belonging to this group also contain one or two copies of the 'A' consensus sequence [[cite:PUB00001131]] or the 'P-loop' sequence motif [[cite:PUB00005361]].

name: ATPase, AAA-type, core
description:
AAA ATPases (ATPases Associated with diverse cellular Activities) form a large protein family and play a number of roles in the cell including cell-cycle regulation, protein proteolysis and disaggregation, organelle biogenesis and intracellular transport. Some of them function as molecular chaperones, subunits of proteolytic complexes or independent proteases (FtsH, Lon). They also act as DNA helicases and transcription factors [[cite:PUB00043605]].

AAA ATPases belong to the AAA+ superfamily of ringshaped P-loop NTPases, which act via the energy-dependent unfolding of macromolecules [[cite:PUB00014779], [cite:PUB00043606]]. There are six major clades of AAA domains (proteasome subunits, metalloproteases, domains D1 and D2 of ATPases with two AAA domains, the MSP1/katanin/spastin group and BCS1 and it homologues), as well as a number of deeply branching minor clades [[cite:PUB00014779]].

They assemble into oligomeric assemblies (often hexamers) that form a ring-shaped structure with a central pore. These proteins produce a molecular motor that couples ATP binding and hydrolysis to changes in conformational states that act upon a target substrate, either translocating or remodelling it [[cite:PUB00033933]].


They are found in all living organisms and share the common feature of the presence of a highly conserved AAA domain called the AAA module. This domain is responsible for ATP binding and hydrolysis. It contains 200-250 residues, among them there are two classical motifs, Walker A (GX4GKT) and Walker B (HyDE) [[cite:PUB00043605]].

name: SLC41A/MgtE, integral membrane domain
description: This entry represents the integral membrane region of SLC41 transporter proteins. SLC41 family of divalent cation transporters includes SLC41A1/2/3  from eukaryotes and  MgtE from prokaryotes [[cite:PUB00098696], [cite:PUB00098704], [cite:PUB00098698], [cite:PUB00098699], [cite:PUB00044725]]. MgtE is the prokaryote homologue of SLC41A1 magnesium transporter which adopts a homodimeric architecture with five transmembrane domains in the C terminus and cytosolic N terminus domains [[cite:PUB00098702], [cite:PUB00044725]]. Bacterial family members have one copy of this region while archaeal and eukaryotic sequences have two.

name: Phospholipid methyltransferase
description: This entry includes Saccharomyces cerevisiae phospholipid methyltransferase [ec:2.1.1.16], which has a broad substrate specificity of unsaturated phospholipids [[cite:PUB00009897]], and related enzymes such as methanethiol S-methyltransferase ([ec:2.1.1.334]), which catalyses the methylation of methanethiol to yield dimethylsulphide (a volatile compound important in climate regulation) [[cite:PUB00089624]].

name: Restriction endonuclease type IV, Mrr
description:
There are four classes of restriction endonucleases: types I, II, III and IV. All types of enzymes recognise specific short DNA sequences and carry out the endonucleolytic cleavage of DNA to give specific double-stranded fragments with terminal 5'-phosphates. They differ in their recognition sequence, subunit composition, cleavage position, and cofactor requirements [[cite:PUB00035705], [cite:PUB00035707]], as summarised below:

Type I enzymes ([ec:3.1.21.3]) cleave at sites remote from recognition site; require both ATP and S-adenosyl-L-methionine to function; multifunctional protein with both restriction and methylase ([ec:2.1.1.72]) activities.
Type II enzymes ([ec:3.1.21.4]) cleave within or at short specific distances from recognition site; most require magnesium; single function (restriction) enzymes independent of methylase.
Type III enzymes ([ec:3.1.21.5]) cleave at sites a short distance from recognition site; require ATP (but doesn't hydrolyse it);  S-adenosyl-L-methionine stimulates reaction but is not required; exists as part of a complex with a modification methylase methylase ([ec:2.1.1.72]).
Type IV enzymes target methylated DNA.

name: ABC transporter type 1, transmembrane domain
description: This entry represents the transmembrane domain in cases where the TMD and ABC region are found in the same protein, and corresponds to ABC type 1 from Transporter Classification Database.

name: SHOCT domain
description: The Short C-terminal (SHOCT) domain is widespread in bacteria but is missing from the most commonly studied model organisms, despite being present in closely related species. It's predominantly C-terminal location, co-occurrence with numerous other domains and short size is reminiscent of the Gram-positive anchor motif, however it is present in a much wider range of species. It may be involved in oligomerisation and nucleic acid binding [[cite:PUB00072411]].

name: Domain of unknown function DUF2510
description: This domain of unknown function is found in a family of proteins conserved in Actinobacteria. Many members are annotated as putative membrane proteins but this could not be confirmed.

name: BREX system ATP-binding protein BrxD
description: This is a family of proteins found in bacterial proteins associated with Bacteriophage Exclusion (BREX) system, a defense system that allows bacteriophage adsorption but blocks phage DNA replication [[cite:PUB00093365]]. This family represents the ATP-binding protein BrxD [[cite:PUB00093365]].

name: SNIPE associated domain
description: This entry represents the SNIPE associated domain, a small largely α-helical domain found in the SNIPE protein family. SNIPE proteins cleave phage DNA during genome injection. This domain may help SNIPE bind to phage DNA.

name: Double zinc ribbon
description: This entry represents a pair of zinc ribbon domains found in diverse sequences including double zinc ribbon and ankyrin repeat-containing protein 1 from Danio rerio (Dzank1) and similar sequences from animals and uncharacterised proteins from bacteria and archaea. Dzank1 is required for the intracellular transport of organelles and vesicles, and is essential for the photoreceptor's outer segments formation, maintenance and function [[cite:PUB00155457]].

name: Zinc-ribbon domain
description: This entry consists of a single zinc ribbon domain. This domain is found in archaeal Large ribosomal subunit protein eL40 covering the full length of the protein. It is also found in Serine/threonine-protein kinase VRK3 found in eukaryotes, covering the N-terminal. In bacteria, this domain is found at the N-terminal in Membrane-associated protein TcaA and Uncharacterized membrane protein YvbJ.

name: Domain of unknown function DUF4429
description: This presumed domain is functionally uncharacterised. It is found in bacteria, archaea and viruses, and is approximately 90 amino acids in length.  This domain is often found in two tandem copies.

name: Domain of unknown function DUF4428
description: This putative zinc finger domain is found in uncharacterised bacterial and viral proteins.

name: YokE-like, PH domain
description: This entry represents the PH domain found in uncharacterised proteins, including Bacillus subtilis YokE.

name: CbbX, AAA lid domain
description: Many AAA+ proteins have a C-terminal α-helical bundle in addition to the AAA core. The functional roles of the helical bundle are varied and include the formation of a lid over the nucleotide binding site and mediation of subunit interactions in oligomeric protein complexes. This entry represents the α helical AAA+ lid domain that is found to the C terminus of AAA domains in the CfxQ/CbbX family [[cite:PUB00091651]].

name: Domain of unknown function DUF7115
description: This domain is found at the N-terminal of HVO_2519 from Haloferax volcanii ([swissprot:D4GTV1]) and similar proteins mainly from halobacteria. It has several conserved motifs RTxxYR, Y/FxHxxxR an RRK. It is predicted to show an α-β configuration.

name: Protein of unknown function DUF8108-like, N-terminal domain
description: This entry represents the N-terminal domain from a family of uncharacterised proteins from halophilic archaea and some bacterial species. Proteins in this family are typically between 150 and 350 amino acids in length. They are predicted to be membrane-associated, as suggested by the presence of two transmembrane helices in the central domain [interpro:IPR058963].

name: ATPase family associated with various cellular activities (AAA)
description: AAA family proteins often perform chaperone-like functions that assist in the assembly, operation, or disassembly of protein complexes [[cite:PUB00005841]].

name: ABC transporter
description: ABC transporters for a large family of proteins responsible for translocation of a variety of compounds across biological membranes.  ABC transporters are the largest family of proteins in many completely sequenced bacteria. ABC transporters are composed of two copies of this domain and two copies of a transmembrane domain [pfam:PF00664]. These four domains may belong to a single polypeptide as in [swissprot:P13569], or belong in different polypeptide chains.

name: CBS domain
description: CBS domains are small intracellular modules that pair together to form a stable globular domain [[cite:PUB00000457]]. This family represents a single CBS domain. Pairs of these domains have been termed a Bateman domain [[cite:PUB00019267]]. CBS domains have been shown to bind ligands with an adenosyl group such as AMP, ATP and S-AdoMet [[cite:PUB00014845]].  CBS domains are found attached to a wide range of other protein domains suggesting that CBS domains may play a regulatory role making proteins sensitive to adenosyl carrying ligands. The region containing the CBS domains in Cystathionine-beta synthase is involved in regulation by S-AdoMet [[cite:PUB00019266]]. CBS domain pairs from AMPK bind AMP or ATP [[cite:PUB00014845]]. The CBS domains from IMPDH and the chloride channel CLC2 bind ATP [[cite:PUB00014845]].

name: ABC transporter transmembrane region
description: This family represents a unit of six transmembrane helices. Many members of the ABC transporter family ([pfam:PF00005]) have two such regions.

name: Divalent cation transporter
description: This region is the integral membrane part of the eubacterial MgtE family of magnesium transporters. Related regions are found also in archaebacterial and eukaryotic proteins. All the archaebacterial and eukaryotic examples have two copies of the region. This suggests that the eubacterial examples may act as dimers. Members of this family probably transport Mg2+ or other divalent cations into the cell. The alignment contains two highly conserved aspartates that may be involved in cation binding (Bateman A unpubl.)

name: Phospholipid methyltransferase
description: The S. cerevisiae phospholipid methyltransferase ([ec:2.1.1.16]) has a broad substrate specificity of unsaturated phospholipids [[cite:PUB00009897]].

name: Restriction endonuclease
description: Prokaryotic family found in type II restriction enzymes containing the hallmark (D/E)-(D/E)XK active site. Presence of catalytic residues implicates this region in the enzymatic cleavage of DNA [[cite:PUB00010098],[cite:PUB00010100]].

name: Protein of unknown function (DUF2510)
description: This is family of proteins conserved in Actinobacteria. Many members are annotated as putative membrane proteins but this could not be confirmed.

name: BREX system ATP-binding protein BrxC/D
description: This is the ATP-binding domain found in proteins that are associated with Bacteriophage Exclusion (BREX) system, a defense system that allows bacteriophage adsorption but blocks phage DNA replication [[cite:PUB00093365]]. This domain is found in the ATP-binding proteins BrxC/pglY (due to its homology with pglY from Pgl system) and BrxD [[cite:PUB00093365]].

name: Double zinc ribbon
description: This family consists of a pair of zinc ribbon domains.

name: zinc-ribbon domain
description: This family consists of a single zinc ribbon domain, ie half of a pair as in family DZR. [pfam:PF12773].

name: SNIPE associated domain
description: This entry represents the SNIPE associated domain a small largely alpha helical domain found in the SNIPE protein family. SNIPE proteins cleave phage DNA during genome injection. This domain may help SNIPE bind to phage DNA.

name: Meiotically up-regulated gene 113
description: This is a family of fungal proteins found to be up-regulated in meiosis.

name: Bacterial PH domain
description: Proteins in this family are distantly related to PH domains.

name: Domain of unknown function (DUF4428)
description: This putative zinc finger domain is found in uncharacterised bacterial proteins.

name: Domain of unknown function (DUF4429)
description: This presumed domain is functionally uncharacterised. This domain family is found in bacteria, archaea and viruses, and is approximately 90 amino acids in length.  This domain is often found in two tandem copies.

name: AAA lid domain
description: This entry represents the alpha helical AAA+ lid domain that is found to the C-terminus of AAA domains.

name: Domain of unknown function (DUF7115)
description: This domain is found at the N-terminal of HVO_2519 from Haloferax volcanii ([swissprot:D4GTV1]) and similar proteins mainly found in halobacteria. It has several conserved motifs RTxxYR, Y/FxHxxxR an RRK. It is predicted to show an alpha-beta configuration.

name: Domain of unknown function (DUF8108) N-terminal domain
description: This entry represents the N-terminal domain from a family of uncharacterised proteins from halophilic archaea. The family is predominantly found in the class Halobacteria, which includes members from the families Natrialbaceae and Haloferacaceae. Proteins in this family are typically between 150 and 350 amino acids in length. They are predicted to be membrane-associated, as suggested by the presence of two transmembrane helices in the central domain.
